case_name: Emergency potable reuse from mixed industrial-municipal water with cyanotoxins, pesticide, and antibiotic under incomplete characterization
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for emergency potable reuse of a partially characterized mixed industrial-municipal source containing cyanotoxin risk, pesticide contamination, and antibiotic residues under rapid deployment constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A coastal city facing a severe drought is considering emergency potable reuse from a blended stream consisting of secondary municipal effluent, storm-driven reservoir transfers, and intermittent discharges from nearby agrochemical formulation and animal-processing industries. Recent screening confirmed microcystins, fenamiphos, and chlortetracycline, but the blend ratio changes daily and only limited sampling is available, so additional transformation products and other trace organics are suspected but not fully characterized. Algal bloom intensity in the contributing reservoir is variable, and utilities need a rapidly scalable treatment train that is robust to short-term spikes while still being defensible for potable reuse.

water_quality.trace_metal_note: no acute metals excursion identified in limited screening; confirmatory sampling incomplete

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should be suitable as potable reuse source water after advanced treatment, providing reliable removal or control of pathogens, microcystins, fenamiphos, chlortetracycline, dissolved organics, and salinity-related constituents while minimizing breakthrough risk during variable influent events.
secondary_goals: support emergency deployment and fast modular scale-up
provide multiple barriers against both known and unknown trace organics
maintain operational robustness during algal bloom and industrial shock events
make uncertainty and residual risk explicit

core_unit_functions.equalization_and_blending: buffer short-term quality spikes and reduce shock loading from variable industrial and bloom-related inputs
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_with_coagulati: remove algae-associated solids, lower organic precursor load, and adsorb a portion of microcystins, fenamiphos, and antibiotic residues
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize taste-odor compounds and many trace organics while improving downstream biodegradability
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: polish biodegradable oxidation byproducts and provide additional adsorption-biodegradation of residual micropollutants
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: provide particulate and microbial barrier and protect RO
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: remove dissolved salts, many low-molecular-weight organics, and provide a major potable reuse barrier
core_unit_functions.UV_advanced_oxidation: provide high-confidence destruction of residual trace organics that may pass RO or be poorly characterized
core_unit_functions.final_stabilization_and_disinfection: restore finished water stability and maintain distribution-compatible microbial protection

applicability_rationale: this is the strongest multi-barrier option for potable reuse when known contaminants include cyanotoxins, pesticide residues, and antibiotics but an unknown contaminant fraction is also suspected
equalization is important because the source blend ratio and bloom severity vary daily and emergency operation increases the risk of short-duration concentration spikes
PAC plus clarification provides relatively rapid deployable front-end risk reduction for algal organics and hydrophobic or moderately adsorbable contaminants before membranes
ozonation followed by BAC is a practical polishing combination for broad-spectrum trace organic control and for managing variable natural organic matter better than adsorption alone

RO and UV-AOP are commonly favored in advanced potable reuse trains because they provide complementary barriers against dissolved contaminants that cannot be confidently excluded by limited source characterization
although complex and energy intensive, this train best matches the potable reuse objective and uncertainty profile
potential_risks: oxidation performance for fenamiphos and chlortetracycline depends on site-specific water matrix effects and should not be assumed without pilot or rapid bench confirmation
microcystin risk can increase if intact cyanobacterial cells are lysed before effective solids removal, so oxidant placement and dose control are critical
bromide is present, creating a non-trivial bromate formation risk during ozonation that must be actively managed
RO concentrate may retain unresolved trace organics and disposal logistics may become a bottleneck during emergency deployment
unknown transformation products from ozonation or AOP may require follow-up bioassay or targeted screening
high operator competency and online monitoring are needed to avoid breakthrough under rapid scale-up conditions

core_unit_functions.equalization_and_blending: reduce influent variability and smooth contaminant peaks
core_unit_functions.dissolved_air_flotation_or_clarification: remove algae and suspended solids while adding rapid adsorption capacity for cyanotoxins and organic micropollutants
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: provide adsorption polishing for microcystins, pesticide residues, and antibiotic compounds
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: provide low-turbidity feed and microbial barrier before RO
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: remove dissolved contaminants and salts to potable reuse quality
core_unit_functions.UV_advanced_oxidation: destroy residual RO-permeable organics and add redundancy for unknowns
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: maintain microbiological safety after advanced treatment

applicability_rationale: this train is attractive when rapid modular deployment is needed because PAC, GAC, UF, and skid-mounted RO/UV units are commercially available and scalable
front-end algae control is particularly important where source water intermittently contains cyanobacterial biomass and dissolved microcystins
the absence of ozonation reduces bromate concerns and may simplify emergency commissioning
adsorption plus membrane barriers can perform well for the named contaminants, but confidence is somewhat lower for very polar or unidentified compounds than in the top-ranked option
potential_risks: GAC exhaustion and PAC dose optimization are highly sensitive to DOC fluctuations and may become difficult under high-variability events
without ozone-BAC, some biodegradable byproducts and poorly adsorbable trace organics may persist until RO or UV-AOP
if algal cells are not adequately separated ahead of adsorption, toxin release episodes may exceed adsorption design assumptions
spent carbon replacement frequency and supply chain reliability are uncertainty points during emergency response
unknown contaminants with low adsorbability but partial RO passage could challenge this train if UV-AOP is underdesigned

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_and_clarificati: remove suspended solids, algae, and some organic precursor material
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: polish particulates and provide a robust microbial barrier
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: remove dissolved organics and salts, with RO favored if potable reuse targets are stringent
core_unit_functions.UV_advanced_oxidation: oxidize residual trace organics and provide another barrier for unknowns

applicability_rationale: this is a simpler advanced treatment route that can be commissioned relatively quickly using established municipal infrastructure plus containerized membrane systems
it can work if adsorption media supply or ozone systems are not feasible in the emergency timeline
RO plus UV-AOP still provides a strong potable reuse barrier set, especially when source characterization is incomplete
however, the lack of dedicated adsorption or biologically active polishing makes upstream contaminant variability more consequential for membrane fouling and unknown dissolved organics
potential_risks: microcystin management is less forgiving if dissolved toxin loads spike because the train relies heavily on downstream barriers rather than strong early adsorption polishing
NF may be insufficiently conservative for some potable reuse scenarios and RO would usually be safer given the uncertain contaminant inventory
membrane fouling risk rises during bloom episodes and with industrial organic fluctuations
without BAC or GAC, there is less resilience against unknown trace contaminants and oxidation byproducts
performance confidence depends strongly on integrity monitoring and frequent verification sampling

core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_with_dissolved: remove algal solids and adsorb a portion of dissolved organics and toxins
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: provide low-turbidity water and microbial control
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_without_RO: oxidize target micropollutants in the absence of desalination
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: polish residual oxidation byproducts and remaining trace organics

applicability_rationale: this route may be considered only if RO deployment is temporarily infeasible due to equipment lead time, concentrate disposal limitations, or emergency power constraints
it uses relatively deployable unit processes and offers meaningful reduction of the named contaminants
it is included as a conditional fallback rather than a preferred solution because potable reuse typically benefits from a stronger dissolved contaminant barrier than oxidation plus carbon alone
potential_risks: without RO, dissolved salts and some low-level unknown organics may not be reduced to a defensible potable reuse margin
AOP can form transformation products that require downstream carbon and verification monitoring
treatment credit for diverse unknown contaminants is less robust than for membrane-based advanced reuse trains
PAC and GAC demand may become excessive during DOC spikes, undermining emergency reliability
this should be treated as an interim contingency option rather than a high-confidence long-term potable reuse train

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_and_sedimentati: remove suspended solids and some algae-associated material

applicability_rationale: this conventional baseline is listed to show a clearly inadequate lower-bound option under the stated potable reuse objective

it may reduce turbidity and some intact cyanobacterial cells, but it does not provide a sufficiently reliable barrier set for dissolved microcystins, fenamiphos, chlortetracycline, salinity, or unknown trace contaminants
potential_risks: dissolved toxin and micropollutant breakthrough risk is unacceptable for potable reuse
chlorination alone is not a dependable strategy for the mixed trace organic profile and may increase by-product concerns
high influent variability and incomplete characterization make conventional treatment-only approaches particularly unsafe
this train does not address TDS or provide advanced barriers expected for potable reuse

preferred_rank_pattern: advanced multi-barrier potable reuse trains with equalization, adsorption or oxidation polishing, membrane separation, and final advanced oxidation should rank above simpler trains
options that explicitly manage algal/cyanotoxin uncertainty and variable industrial contributions should rank higher than membrane-only or conventional-only approaches
RO-based trains should generally outrank non-RO alternatives because the source is only partially characterized and potable reuse demands strong dissolved contaminant control

must_reflect: emergency deployment and rapid scale-up favor modular and commercially mature unit processes
mixed contaminants and unknown fraction require more than one barrier type rather than reliance on a single mechanism
high influent variability requires buffering and front-end solids or algae management
residuals, spent carbon logistics, and concentrate handling tradeoffs should be acknowledged explicitly

must_contain: why early management of intact cyanobacterial cells matters before oxidation steps
why adsorption and or biological polishing are useful for microcystins, pesticide residues, and antibiotics under uncertain co-contaminant conditions
why RO plus UV-AOP provides a stronger potable reuse safety margin when some contaminants are only suspected and not fully characterized
why conventional treatment alone is insufficient for dissolved trace organics and potable reuse assurance

should_mention: high day-to-day blend variability and incomplete contaminant characterization
possible toxin release from cell lysis if oxidants are misplaced relative to solids removal
bromate and oxidation by-product formation risk during ozone or advanced oxidation
carbon exhaustion uncertainty under DOC spikes and emergency supply-chain stress
membrane fouling, concentrate management, and residual unknowns despite advanced treatment
the need for rapid bench or pilot confirmation because named compounds may not represent the full risk profile
